coredata.dc:title: Experimental Methods to Study Environmental Sustainability of Silicon-based Lithium Ion Battery Manufacturing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: Sustainable Manufacturing for Global Circular Economy: Proceedings of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fenfen
Ma, Lulu
Yuan, Chris
coredata.dc:description: Next-generation lithium ion batteries (LIBs) are under rapid development using various nano-structured materials. Among them, silicon nanowires (SiNWs) and silicon nanotubes (SiNTs) are two promising anode materials due to their high specific capacities. However, fabrication processes of SiNWs and SiNTs involve a large amount of toxic chemicals and generate significant potential impacts on the environment. In this study, both in-situ and ex-situ measurements were proposed for investigating the process emissions, including gas, aerosol nanoparticle, and aqueous nanoparticle emissions, from chemical etching processes for producing SiNWs and SiNTs for use as anode materials for LIBs. The feasibilities of the measurements were verified by the preliminary results of the emissions from the SiNTs synthesis process. This study provides insight into experimental measurements of and supports the gathering of process data on emissions from synthesis processes for SiNWs and SiNTs as anode materials for LIBs, which can be useful for future assessment of the sustainability performance of silicon-based LIB technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium ion batteries
Silicon nanowires
Silicon nanotubes
Process emissions
Sustainability performance

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Sustainable Manufacturing for Global Circular Economy: Proceedings of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Session 22: Sustainable Manufacturing Processes - Manufacturing Processes, Tools and Equipment

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirectÂ Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia * Paulo Afonso. Tel.: +351 253 510 761; fax: +351 253 604 741 E-mail address: psafonso@dps.uminho.pt 2351-9789 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Manufacturing Engineering Society International Conference 2017. Manufacturing Engineering Society International Conference 2017, MESIC 2017, 28-30 June 2017, Vigo (Pontevedra), Spain Costing models for capacity optimization in Industry 4.0: Trade-off between used capacity and operational efficiency A. Santanaa, P. Afonsoa,*, A. Zaninb, R. Wernkeb a University of Minho, 4800-058 GuimarÃ£es, Portugal bUnochapecÃ³, 89809-000 ChapecÃ³, SC, Brazil Abstract Under the concept of "Industry 4.0", production processes will be pushed to be increasingly interconnected, information based on a real time basis and, necessarily, much more efficient. In this context, capacity optimization goes beyond the traditional aim of capacity maximization, contributing also for organizationâ€™s profitability and value. Indeed, lean management and continuous improvement approaches suggest capacity optimization instead of maximization. The study of capacity optimization and costing models is an important research topic that deserves contributions from both the practical and theoretical perspectives. This paper presents and discusses a mathematical model for capacity management based on different costing models (ABC and TDABC). A generic model has been developed and it was used to analyze idle capacity and to design strategies towards the maximization of organizationâ€™s value. The trade-off capacity maximization vs operational efficiency is highlighted and it is shown that capacity optimization might hide operational inefficiency. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Manufacturing Engineering Society International Conference 2017. Keywords: Cost Models; ABC; TDABC; Capacity Management; Idle Capacity; Operational Efficiency 1. Introduction The cost of idle capacity is a fundamental information for companies and their management of extreme importance in modern production systems. In general, it is defined as unused capacity or production potential and can be measured in several ways: tons of production, available hours of manufacturing, etc. The management of the idle capacity Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/lice ses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing (GCSM). 10.1016/j.promfg.2019.04.062 10.1016/j.promfg.2019.04.062 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Publish d by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing (GCSM). Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 2351-9789 Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing (GCSM) 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing - Sustainable Manufacturing for Global Circular Economy Experimental Methods to Study Environmental Sustainability of Silicon-based Lithium Ion Battery Manufacturing Fenfen Wanga, Lulu Mab, Chris Yuana,* aDepartment of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Case Wester Reserve University, Cleveland, OH 44106 bDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee, Milwaukee, WI 53211 Abstract Next-generation lithium ion batteries (LIBs) are under rapid development using various nano-structured materials. Among them, silicon nanowires (SiNWs) and silicon nanotubes (SiNTs) are two promising anode materials due to their high specific capacities. However, fabrication processes of SiNWs and SiNTs involve a large amount of toxic chemicals and generate significant potential impacts on the environment. In this study, both in-situ and ex-situ measurements were proposed for investigating the process emissions, including gas, aerosol nanoparticle, and aqueous nanoparticle emissions, from chemical etching processes for producing SiNWs and SiNTs for use as anode materials for LIBs. The feasibilities of the measurements were verified by the preliminary results of the emissions from the SiNTs synthesis process. This study provides insight into experimental measurements of and supports the gathering of process data on emissions from synthesis processes for SiNWs and SiNTs as anode materials for LIBs, which can be useful for future assessment of the sustainability performance of silicon-based LIB technology. Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing (GCSM) Keywords: Lithium ion batteries; Silicon nanowires; Silicon nanotubes; Process emissions; Sustainability performance * Corresponding author. Tel.: +1-216-368-5191; fax: +1-216-368-6445. E-mail address: chris.yuan@case.edu Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Sci nceDir t Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 2351-9789 Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an ope acces article under CC BY-NC-ND lic nse (https://creativec mmo s.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing (GCSM) 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing - Sustainable Manufacturing for Global Circular Economy Experimental Methods to Study Environmental Sustainability of Silicon-based Lithium Ion Battery Manufacturing Fenfen Wanga, Lulu Mab, Chris Yuana,* aDepartment of Mechanical d Aerospac Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cl veland, OH 44106 bDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee, Milwaukee, WI 53211 Abstract Next-generation lith um ion batter es (LIBs) ar under rapid development using various n no-structured materials. Among them, silicon nanowires (SiNWs) and silicon nanotubes (SiNTs) ar two pro ising anode materials due to their high spec fic capacities. However, fabrication processes of SiNWs and SiNTs nvolve a large amount of toxic ch micals an generat si n ficant potential i pacts on the environment. In this study, both in-situ and ex-situ measur ment were pr posed for investigating th process emissio s, including gas, aerosol nanoparticle, nd aqueous nanoparticle emissions, fro chemical etching processes for oducing SiNWs and SiNTs for use as anode material for LIBs. The f asibilit es of the measurements were verified by the pr liminary results of the emissions f om the SiNTs synthesis process. Thi study r vides insight into experiment l measurements of and supports t e gath ring of process dat on e issi ns from synthesis processes for SiNWs a d SiNTs as anode materials for LIBs, which can be useful for future assessment of the sustainability performance of silicon-based LIB technology. Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an ope acces article under CC BY-NC-ND lic nse (https://creativec mmo s.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee f the 16th Global Conference on Sustainable Manufacturing (GCSM) Keywords: Lithium ion batteries; Silicon nanowires; Silicon nanotubes; Process emissions; Sustainability performance * Corresponding author. Tel.: +1-216-368-5191; fax: +1-216-368-6445. E-mail address: chris.yuan@case.edu 502 Fenfen Wang et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 2 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 1. Introduction Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used in portable electronics, power tools, and electric vehicles because of their high-energy and high-power density, long cycle life, and low self-discharge property [1]. Because of the advantages of high specific capacity (4200 mAh/g), abundance in nature, and environmental friendliness, silicon (Si) has been investigated as a promising high-capacity anode material for LIBs to replace the commercial graphite anodes, so that LIBs can meet the increasing demand on advanced energy storage systems for large-scale applications [2]. The drastic volume change of bulk Si (around 360% for Li4.4Si) during charge-discharge process, however, leads to the fracture of Si particles, electrode collapse, unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), and irreversible capacity loss, and thus limits siliconâ€™s practical applications in commercial LIBs [3]. To tackle these disadvantages, tremendous efforts have been made to develop nano-structured Si such as silicon nanowires (SiNWs) [4], silicon nanotubes (SiNTs) [5], and silicon nano-porous particles [6]. Nano-structured Si materials are capable of providing the necessary free volume for accommodating the large volume expansion of Si and of providing the large surface area for promoting the lithium diffusion, thus improving LIB performance [7]. Among the nano-structured Si materials, SiNWs and SiTNs have been regarded as the two most promising anode materials for next-generation LIBs due to their high-specific capacity, which is enabled by their cylindrical shapes that provide continuous electronic pathways and thus facilitate efficient charge transport [8]. Various methods have been used for fabricating SiNWs and SiNTs, such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [9- 10], vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) growth [11], liquid-synthetic reactions [12], chemical etching [13-14], etc. Among these methods, chemical etching is a simple, low-cost, and scalable process and has a high potential for the future large-scale fabricating of SiNWs and SiNTs for next-generation LIB applications. Despite the advantages, chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs entail such disadvantages as the heavy usage of chemicals and the production of waste and emissions, including nanoparticle emissions. Specifically, nanoparticles have been found to be more toxic than their bulk counterparts due to their special characteristics such as high concentrations, wide size distributions, multiple morphologies and compositions, and different types (e.g. airborne and aqueous particles) [15]. For example, silver nitrate (AgNO3), commonly used as the metal catalyst in chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs, is the major source of silver nanoparticles, which have been shown to have toxic effects on human health [16]. Therefore, the potential environmental impacts of chemical etching processes for producing SiNWs and SiNTs would be significant considering their industrial-scale applications in LIBs. Currently, there are limited studies on the sustainability performance of LIBs with nanostructured material anodes due to the lack of data [17] and there are few studies focusing on the environmental impacts from the experimental scale manufacturing processes for SiNW and SiNT anodes. As next-generation LIBs are under rapid development with silicon-based nano-structured materials (SiNWs and SiNTs), it is necessary to investigate the potential emissions from the nano-structured material manufacturing and establish an inventory of emissions for future decision support in sustainable development of the next-generation LIB technology. In this study, SiNWs and SiNTs for use as anode materials for LIBs were synthesized by chemical etching of silicon. The potential emissions from the synthesis processes were categorized into three types: gaseous emissions, aerosol nanoparticle emissions, and aqueous nanoparticle emissions. Both in-situ and ex-situ measurements were proposed to characterize the potential emissions in order to establish the emission inventory of SiNWs-and SiNTs- based LIB manufacturing. This study provides insight into experimental measurements of and supports the gathering of process data on emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs as anode materials for LIBs, which can be useful for future assessment of the sustainability performance of silicon-based LIB technology. 2. Methods 2.1. Syntheses of SiNWs and SiNTs Chemical etching of silicon was applied for the syntheses of SiNWs and SiNTs, and the detailed procedures are: N-type Si (100) wafers were cleaned by ultrasonication in acetone (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA), and deionized water (DI-H2O), and dried under nitrogen blowing. Before the Fenfen Wang et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 503 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 etching steps, the cleaned Si wafers were dipped into diluted hydrofluoric acid (HF, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) for 2 min to remove the oxide layer. For the synthesis of SiNWs, the Si wafers were immersed in the first etching solution consisting of 5 M HF and 20 mM AgNO3 (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) for 1 min. The Ag-deposited Si wafers were rinsed with DI- H2O and then immersed in the second etching solution containing 4.8 M HF and 0.3 M hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) for 60 min. The etched Si wafers were then dipped into diluted nitric acid (HNO3, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) to remove the residual Ag particles. For the synthesis of SiNTs, the first etching step was completed in 5 M HF and 5 mM AgNO3 for 1 min (step 1), and the second etching step was in 5 M HF and 0.6 M H2O2 for 60 min (step 2). The residual Ag particles were removed with 5 M HNO3 (step 3). The etched Si wafers were then immersed into 1.2 mM cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2, Sigma-Aldrich) and 20 wt% ammonium fluoride (NH4F, Sigma-Aldrich) for 30 min (step 4). Finally, the Si wafers were dipped into 5 M HNO3 solution for removing all the residual chemicals (step 5). The etching processes were conducted in a 50 Â°C water bath. The SiNWs and SiNTs can be collected by ultrasonication in DI-H2O and dried at 60 Â°C. Fig. 1 presents the synthesis processes for SiNWs and SiNTs. Fig. 1. Synthesis processes for SiNWs and SiNTs. 2.2. Proposed measurements for potential emissions The potential emissions from chemical etching processes for synthesizing SiNWs and SiNTs include gaseous emissions, aerosol nanoparticle emissions, and aqueous nanoparticle emissions. The gaseous emissions can be attributed to the second etch step, for which the HF + H2O2 etching solution is used. The corresponding chemical reactions associated with the etching process can be expressed as following: 4 2 + 4 (g) + 2 (g)Si HF SiF Hâ†’ (1) 4 2 6 + HF SiF H SiFâ†’ (2) 2 2 2 6 2 2 + 6 + + 2 + 2 (g)Si HF H O H SiF H O Hâ†’ (3) It can be seen that both SiF4 and H2 gases are produced during the chemical etching of Si for SiNWs and SiNTs (Equation (1)); however, SiF4 can dissolve in the HF solution to form water-soluble H2SiF6 (Equation (2)), leaving H2 as the major gaseous emission (Equation (3)). Additionally, for the synthesis of SiNTs, NH4F was added in step 4, of which the hydrolysis can form HF as expressed by Equation (4). Thus, the etching process can be realized and H2 will be emitted from this step according to Equation (1). In summary, in the chemical etching of Si for SiNWs, H2 emissions are mainly generated from the second etching step (HF + H2O2); for SiNTs, H2 emissions can be generated from both step 2 (HF + H2O2) and step 4 (Co(NO3)2 + NH4F). 4 2 3 2F + + NH H O HF NH H Oï‚« ïƒ— (4) 504 Fenfen Wang et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 4 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 As a result, regarding the gaseous emissions from the chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs, H2 emissions will be the major concern. To test the real-time gaseous emissions, an in-situ measurement using a residual gas analyzer (RGA) can be used. RGA is a mass spectrometer measuring partial pressures of gases, from which the concentrations of gases can be obtained by using the general gas equation and Avogadroâ€™s law [18]. In addition to the gaseous emissions, large amounts of nanoparticle emissions, including both aerosol and aqueous nanoparticle emissions, can also be generated in the chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs. The environmental impacts of nanoparticle emissions have been demonstrated to be correlated with a number of nanoparticle parameters [19], including concentrations, size distributions, morphologies, material compositions, etc. To characterize the nanoparticle parameters, the investigation of nanoparticle emissions from the chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs will be conducted using both in-situ and ex-situ measurements. The in-situ measurements of aerosol nanoparticles are to identify real-time aerosol nanoparticle emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs and to provide such parameters as nanoparticle concentrations and size distributions, for establishing the nanoparticle emission inventory. Specifically, number concentrations of the aerosol nanoparticle emissions can be measured by an Ultrafine Condensation Particle Counter (UCPC, TSI 3776), which is a continuous-flow instrument and can measure concentrations up to 300,000 particles/cm3. The working principle of the UCPC involves three processes: (1) supersaturation of the working fluid (for model 3776, it is butanol), (2) growth of particles by condensation of vapors, and (3) detection of particles [20]. Size distributions of the aerosol nanoparticle emissions can be tested by a Scanning Mobility Particle Sizer (SMPS, TSI 3936). The SMPS consists of an electrostatic classifier (TSI 3080) and UCPC, and performs particle-size classification based on differential electrical mobility classification. The ex-situ measurements of nanoparticle emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs will be employed to characterize the nanoparticle parameters that cannot be measured through the in-situ measurements. For the ex-situ measurements, concentrations of the aqueous nanoparticle emissions can be determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), which is an analytical technique used for elemental determination by combining an ICP source (converting the elemental atoms in the sample to ions) with a mass spectrometer (separating and detecting the ions) [21]. Size distributions of the aqueous nanoparticle emissions can be tested by a ZetaPlus Zeta Potential and Particle Size Analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Corp.), which can provide particle size distributions in liquid suspensions by following the principle of dynamic light scattering [22]. To obtain such parameters as particle size, shape, aspect ratio, etc., morphologies of the collected nanoparticle samples will be characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H 9000 NR). To determine the elemental compositions of the nanoparticle emissions, Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, QUANTAX EDS) attached to the SEM system will be used. All the measured data will be collected for the establishment of nanoparticle emission inventory for silicon-based LIB manufacturing. The ex-situ measurements of nanoparticle emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs will be useful in understanding the nanoparticle emission mechanism, and the established nanoparticle emission inventory can facilitate the environmental impact and health risk assessments and provide decision support in sustainable development of silicon-based next-generation LIB technology. Fig. 2 shows the proposed in-situ and ex-situ measurements of emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs. Fig. 2. Proposed measurements of emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs. Fenfen Wang et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 505 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 2.3. Fabrications of SiNW and SiNT electrodes LIB coin cells with lithium metal foils as counter electrodes can be fabricated in an Ar-filled glovebox. SiNW- and SiNT-based anode materials can be obtained by mixing the as-synthesized SiNWs and SiNTs, carbon black, and alginate binder in a certain weight ratio to form a uniform slurry. The mixed materials can be coated on copper films and dried in a vacuum oven to form the working electrodes. 1.0 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate combined with vinylene carbonate can be used as electrolyte. 3. Preliminary results and discussion 3.1. Morphologies of SiNWs and SiNTs The morphologies of SiNWs and SiNTs were characterized by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. The upper parts of the SiNWs stuck together and form bundles of SiNWs (Fig. 3a), which can be ascribed to surface tension forces exerted on the SiNWs during the drying process [23]. The lateral view of the SiNWs (Fig. 3b) indicates that the SiNWs have an average length of 8 Âµm. Fig. 3c and 3d present the top and lateral views of the SiNTs, and the results reveal that the SiNTs have goblet shapes and possess sizes around 15 Âµm long and 2 Âµm wide. The results also indicate that both SiNWs and SiNTs are perpendicular to the substrates, a phenomenon that can be attributed to the n-type Si (100) wafers and the preferred [100] etching direction induced by the etching solution dissolution of Si [24]. Fig. 3. SEM images of SiNWs and SiNTs. (a, b) top and lateral views of SiNWs; (c, d) top and lateral views of SiNTs. Electrochemical performances of SiNW and SiNT electrodes can be tested using galvanostatic discharge-charge tests, from which the specific capacities of SiNW and SiNT electrodes can be obtained. The performances of the SiNW and SiNT electrodes can be tested on a LANHE CT2001A battery tester (Land, China). At 100 mA g-1, of the SiNT electrode, the initial discharge and charge capacities are 2675 and 1610 mA h g-1, respectively [18]. At 50mAg-1, the initial discharge capacity of the SiNW electrode is greater than 3000 mA h g-1 and charge capacity is greater than 2500 mA h g-1. 3.2. Gaseous emissions As mentioned above, the major gas emissions during the synthesis processes for SiNWs and SiNTs are the H2 emissions, which can be tested by in-situ RGA measurement. To collect the gaseous samples, a fused silica capillary 506 Fenfen Wang et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 6 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 connected to the RGA system can be hung over the surface of the etching solution. From the RGA test, gases including H2, CO2, N2, O2, Ar, and H2O can be detected, among which the concentration percentage of H2 can be obtained. Then, the amounts of H2 emissions can be calculated according to the ideal gas law. After knowing the specific capacities of SiNW and SiNT electrodes, amounts of H2 emissions based on 1 mAh of electrodes can be evaluated. For example, for 1 mAh of SiNT electrode, a total of 10.93 mg of H2 emission is generated from the chemical etching process, among which 9.44 mg of H2 are generated from step 2 and 1.49 mg of H2 from step 4. 3.3. Aerosol nanoparticle emissions The collection of aerosol nanoparticle samples for the in-situ UCPC measurement can be similar to that of gaseous emissions for the in-situ RGA test. The UCPC measurement provides the real-time variations of number concentrations of aerosol nanoparticles with time, from which the total number concentrations of aerosol nanoparticle emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs can be obtained. After knowing the capacities of SiNW and SiNT electrodes, the concentrations of aerosol nanoparticle emissions for 1 mAh of SiNW and SiNT electrodes can be evaluated. For example, for 1 mAh of SiNT electrode, the total number concentration of aerosol nanoparticle emissions is 1.92 Ã— 107 particles/cm3. Specifically, step 1 produces a number of 1.84 Ã— 107 particles/cm3, which make up about 95.5% of the total aerosol nanoparticle emissions. Aerosol nanoparticle concentrations from steps 2 and 4 are 8.39 Ã— 105 and 1.80 Ã—104 particles/cm3, respectively. For the SEM and TEM ex-situ measurements of the aerosol nanoparticle emissions, the samples can be collected by attaching carbon tapes and copper grids to the parafilm that covered reaction containers. The aerosol nanoparticles will be deposited on the carbon tapes and copper grids, which can then be transferred to the SEM and TEM equipment, respectively. From our previous work [18], particle agglomeration phenomenon is observed in the aerosol nanoparticle emissions, and the results reveal that different steps generate different sizes of nanoparticles. 3.4. Aqueous nanoparticle emissions The aqueous nanoparticle emissions from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs will also be characterized for morphologies, chemical compositions, concentrations, and size distributions. Such parameters can be obtained by using ex-situ measurements. For the SEM and TEM ex-situ measurements of nanoparticle morphologies, the aqueous nanoparticle samples can be collected by dripping small droplets of the particle- containing solutions on carbon tapes and copper grids, which can then be transferred to the SEM and TEM equipment after the samples had dried. As described above, chemical compositions of the aqueous nanoparticles can be verified by EDS, concentrations can be determined by ICP-MS, and size distributions can be tested by ZetaPlus. It has been found that from the synthesis process for the SiNTs, both individual particles and large particle agglomerates coexist in the aqueous nanoparticle emissions. Specifically, small spheres attached to larger ones are observed in step 1; small spheres aggregated together, in step 2; large rhombuses covered by small spheres, in step 3; cubic particles, in step 4; and irregular particles, in step 5 [18]. Elemental compositions of the aqueous nanoparticles from the SiNTs synthesis process indicate that the elements N, O and Si are found in all steps, while the elements Co, F and Ag only in certain steps. For 1 mAh of SiNT electrode, total concentrations of the aqueous nanoparticle emissions, including Ag, Si and Co nanoparticles, from the synthesis process for the SiNTs are evaluated. The results reveal that the total concentrations of aqueous nanoparticle emissions include 6.20 mg of Ag, 188.39 mg of Si, and 1.69 mg of Co. In summary, the investigations of emissions from the chemical etching process for SiNTs demonstrate the feasibilities of the proposed in-site and ex-situ measurements, which provide insight into experimental measurements of emissions from SiNWs and SiNTs manufacturing for use as anode materials for LIBs. 4. Conclusion Both in-situ and ex-situ measurements were proposed for characterizing the process emissions, including gases, aerosol nanoparticle, and aqueous nanoparticle emissions, from chemical etching processes for SiNWs and SiNTs for use as anode materials for LIBs. The feasibilities of these measurements were demonstrated by the preliminary Fenfen Wang et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 33 (2019) 501â€“507 507 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2018) 000â€“000 7 results of the emissions from the SiNTs synthesis process. This study provides insight into experimental measurements of and supports the gathering of process data on emissions from chemical etching processes for fabrications of SiNWs and SiNTs for LIBs, which can be useful for future assessment of the sustainability performances of SiNWs-and SiNTs-based LIB technologies. Acknowledgements Financial support from National Science Foundation (CBET-1744031) is gratefully acknowledged. References [1] P. Roy, S. K. Gonzalez-Rodriguez, J. L. Coffer, T. Djenizian, Self-Supported Silicon Nanotube Arrays as an Anode Electrode for Li-Ion Batteries, ECS Transaction, 77 (2017) 349â€“350. [6] X. F. Gao, J. Y. Li, Y. Y. Xie, D. S. Guan, C. Yuan, A multilayered silicon-reduced graphene oxide electrode for high performance lithium- ion batteries, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 7 (2015) 7855â€“7862. [7] J.-Y. Li, Q. Xu, G. Li, Y.-X. Yin, L.-J. Wan, Y.-G. Guo, Research progress regarding Si-based anode materials towards practical application in high energy density Li-ion batteries, Materials Chemistry Frontiers, 2017. [8] X.X. Zuo, J. Zhu, M.B. Muller, Y.J. Cheng, Silicon based lithium-ion battery anodes: a chronicle perspective review, Nano Energy, 31 (2017) 113-143. [9] J. V. Wittemann, W. MÃ¼nchgesang, S. Senz, V. Schmidt, Silver catalyzed ultrathin silicon nanowires grown by low-temperature chemical- vapor-deposition, AIP, 2010. [10] R. Epur, P. J. Hanumantha, M. K. Datta, D. Hong, B. Gattu, P. N. Kumta, A simple and scalable approach to hollow silicon nanotube (h- SiNT) anode architectures of superior electrochemical stability and reversible capacity, Journal of Materials Chemistry A, 3 (2015) 11117â€“ 11129. [11] C.W. Pinion, J. D. Christesen, J. F. Cahoon, Understanding the vaporâ€“liquidâ€“solid mechanism of Si nanowire growth and doping to synthetically encode precise nanoscale morphology, Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 4 (2016) 3890-3897. head.ce:title: Experimental Methods to Study Environmental Sustainability of Silicon-based Lithium Ion Battery Manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, OH 44106

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee, Milwaukee, WI 53211

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Next-generation lithium ion batteries (LIBs) are under rapid development using various nano-structured materials. Among them, silicon nanowires (SiNWs) and silicon nanotubes (SiNTs) are two promising anode materials due to their high specific capacities. However, fabrication processes of SiNWs and SiNTs involve a large amount of toxic chemicals and generate significant potential impacts on the environment. In this study, both in-situ and ex-situ measurements were proposed for investigating the process emissions, including gas, aerosol nanoparticle, and aqueous nanoparticle emissions, from chemical etching processes for producing SiNWs and SiNTs for use as anode materials for LIBs. The feasibilities of the measurements were verified by the preliminary results of the emissions from the SiNTs synthesis process. This study provides insight into experimental measurements of and supports the gathering of process data on emissions from synthesis processes for SiNWs and SiNTs as anode materials for LIBs, which can be useful for future assessment of the sustainability performance of silicon-based LIB technology.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanostructured anode materials for lithium ion batteries
